advantages: Learns representations automatically from raw or lightly processed data, reducing manual feature engineering.
Scales with data and compute — larger models and datasets often yield better performance on complex unstructured tasks.
Supports end-to-end training from input to output with a single differentiable pipeline.
Flexible architectures (CNN, RNN, Transformer, etc.) adapt to vision, language, audio, and multimodal problems.
Powers state-of-the-art results across perception, language, and generative tasks.
disadvantages: Typically needs large labeled datasets and substantial compute (GPUs, memory, training time).
Models can be opaque — learned representations are harder to interpret than explicit hand-crafted features.
Risk of overfitting and brittle behavior when data are scarce, biased, or out-of-distribution.
Deployment cost: inference latency, model size, and serving infrastructure exceed many classical ML solutions.
Not always the best choice for small tabular problems where trees or linear models are faster and more interpretable.
